critic_raw:
This design compares a hybrid CNN-LSTM against logistic regression for readmission prediction using structured EHR data from 10,000 chronic disease patients.

The most important flaw is the feature parity problem. The CNN-LSTM processes temporal sequences of clinical events from a 180-day look-back window. Logistic regression is trained on 'the identical feature set.' If logistic regression literally receives the same temporal sequence features as the CNN-LSTM (i.e., a flattened version of the 180-day sequence), then logistic regression is an underpowered representation of a sequential model, not a clinically meaningful baseline. If logistic regression instead receives only the aggregate summaries (rolling counts, last-admission recency), the comparison conflates temporal granularity access with model architecture. The design does not specify which of these two configurations applies, which makes the experimental conclusion ambiguous regardless of results.

The success criterion specifies '≥0.03 absolute improvement' in AUROC. This threshold is stated without a power calculation. Whether 0.03 AUROC is detectable with high probability on a 2,500-patient test set (estimated from the ~25% test allocation of 10,000 patients) depends on the actual AUROC values and the correlation between model predictions. If both models achieve AUROCs in the 0.70–0.80 range (typical for readmission tasks), a 0.03 difference requires thousands of test samples for adequate power at α=0.05. The design should have included a power analysis to verify that the success criterion is detectable at the available sample size, but does not.

The hypothesis concerns chronic disease patients specifically. The 10,000-patient cohort is described as 'chronic disease patients' but no disease-specific analysis is planned. If the cohort contains multiple chronic diseases (heart failure, COPD, diabetes, CKD), readmission dynamics differ substantially across these populations. The aggregate AUROC will be dominated by the largest disease subgroup. A CNN-LSTM that excels for heart failure but performs at baseline for COPD will still show an aggregate gain, which would be misinterpreted as a general advantage.
all_issues_raised: The design states logistic regression is trained on 'the identical feature set' as the CNN-LSTM but does not specify whether this is the full temporal sequence (flattened) or aggregate summaries — this ambiguity makes the experimental conclusion uninterpretable regardless of results.
The 0.03 AUROC success threshold is stated without a power calculation; at typical readmission AUROC values (0.70-0.80) with a ~2,500-patient test set, this difference may not be reliably detectable.

The cohort encompasses multiple chronic disease types with different readmission dynamics; aggregate AUROC will be dominated by the largest disease subgroup, and disease-specific subgroup analyses are not specified.
all_issues_adjudicated: The design states logistic regression is trained on 'the identical feature set' as the CNN-LSTM but does not specify whether this is the full temporal sequence (flattened) or aggregate summaries — this ambiguity makes the experimental conclusion uninterpretable regardless of results.
The 0.03 AUROC success threshold is stated without a power calculation; at typical readmission AUROC values (0.70-0.80) with a ~2,500-patient test set, this difference may not be reliably detectable.

The cohort encompasses multiple chronic disease types with different readmission dynamics; aggregate AUROC will be dominated by the largest disease subgroup, and disease-specific subgroup analyses are not specified.
